nodes.label: Fan-Out Wafer-Level Packaging (FOWLP)

nodes.label: Nepes

nodes.label: Powertech Technology Inc (PTI)

nodes.label: Samsung Electro-Mechanics (SEMCO)

nodes.label: JCET Group

nodes.label: Deca Technologies

nodes.label: TSMC InFO

nodes.label: Nanium

nodes.label: Epitaxial Growth System

nodes.label: Wafer Fab Process Flow
nodes.description: Pattern Transfer -> Development/Etching -> Annealing, with Photo resist, Thin-film growth; leads to Packaging, Product testing, Completed product.

nodes.label: JMA Tree of Vacuum (Industry-Supporting Vacuum Technology)
nodes.description: Japan Machinery Association (Shinkunoki / vacuum machinery) visualization titled 'Vacuum technology that supports industry' / 'Sangyo wo sasaeru shinku gijutsu'. Branches of the tree classify vacuum-equipment applications into: IT-related (semiconductor back-end, wafer manufacturing / FOPLP / chiplets / advanced packaging, next-gen semiconductor tech, 5G/6G, autonomous driving, metaverse, smart grid), Energy/Environment (space, fuel-cells, batteries, renewables, nuclear simulation), Devices (LED/LD/EL, lenses/mirrors, SSL, quantum dots, sensors, power devices), Display (LCD/PDP/OLED, micro-LED, QD-OLED/QD-EL, HDD/CD/DVD), Bio (bioprinting / mTAS / biofuels), Ceramics / Perovskites / solar cells, Chemistry / Recording media, Food (vacuum packaging, vacuum cooking, vacuum frying, aseptic filling, freeze-drying), Printing, Metal (sputter targets / MEMS sensors / ultra-high-vacuum welding / nanodiamond coating), Civil engineering / Construction (new glass, ZEH/ZEB, super-thin glass), Optics (3D printing, holography, nanophotonics, XCCD), Machinery / Automotive (fuel-cell cars, engines), Vacuum systems (robots)). Root trunk reads 'Vacuum technology that supports industry'.

nodes.label: DRAM Manufacture Flow (Introduction)
nodes.description: Process-flow illustration titled '(2) Introduction to DRAM Manufacture Flow' showing sequential steps: wafers/silicon crystal -> Mask 1 -> N-Well Implant -> Oxidation Furnace -> LPCVD Furnace -> Mask 2 -> Oxidation Furnace x3 -> LPCVD Furnace -> Mask 3 -> Plasma Etch -> LPCVD Furnace -> One Furnace -> LPCVD Furnace -> Mask 4 -> P+ Implant -> Mask 5 -> N+ Implant -> LPCVD Furnace -> APCVD -> Mask 6 -> Mask 7 -> Metal Sputter -> Mask 8 -> PECVD -> Repeat 7-a to 7-d -> Metal Sputter -> Mask 10 -> PECVD -> Mask 11 -> Alloy Furnace -> Wafer Probe.

nodes.label: Ajinomoto Co., Inc.
nodes.description: Japanese multinational parent company of Ajinomoto Fine-Techno. Originally known for MSG (monosodium glutamate) production; diversified into specialty chemicals and electronic materials leveraging amino acid chemistry know-how.

nodes.label: Ajinomoto Build-up Film (ABF)
nodes.description: World's first film produced from liquid resin; interlayer insulating material for semiconductor packaging launched in 1999. Key attributes: easy to form uniform-thickness insulation layers, highly heat resistant, flame retardant, mechanically strong when cured, enables copper microcircuit fabrication via plating (L/S=10/10 um), high insulation and temperature cycle reliability. Manufactured through low-waste environment-friendly process. Supports build-up method of multilayer PCB manufacturing with laser vias.

nodes.label: ABF Low Dielectric Tangent (High-Frequency) Variant
nodes.description: ABF variant launched to market to achieve low dielectric tangent (low Df) for high-frequency applications such as 5G communications and networking. Developed for expanding server and communication applications.

nodes.label: ABF Fine-Line (L/S=10/10 um) Variant
nodes.description: ABF grade enabling fine copper patterning at line/space = 10/10 micrometers via semi-additive plating process; used in advanced high-density build-up substrates.

nodes.label: Monosodium Glutamate (MSG)
nodes.description: Seasoning product; synthesis R&D in the 1950s-1960s generated intermediates (glutamate from soybeans/wheat, chlorinated paraffin from caustic soda/chlorine) that were repurposed into the epoxy resin curing agent business and ultimately ABF.

nodes.label: Chlorinated Paraffin
nodes.description: Chemical intermediate historically produced by Ajinomoto from caustic soda/salt/chlorine feedstocks; part of functional chemicals portfolio (flame retardants).

nodes.label: Activated Carbon & Adsorption Resin
nodes.description: Product line of Ajinomoto Fine-Techno Activated Carbon Division (adsorbents used for water/air/chemical purification).

nodes.label: F-LINE Corporation
nodes.description: Coating contractor for ABF located in Niigata Prefecture, Japan. Part of Ajinomoto's asset-light outsourced coating network.

nodes.label: ABF Coating Contractor (Gunma Prefecture)
nodes.description: Second coating contractor for ABF located in Gunma Prefecture near Ajinomoto Fine-Techno's Gunma Plant. Increases production capacity and provides risk diversification.

nodes.label: Ajinomoto Fine-Techno Gunma Plant
nodes.description: Varnish production plant in Gunma Prefecture. Added for increased ABF production capacity and risk diversification.

nodes.label: Ajinomoto Fine-Techno HQ (Gunma)
nodes.description: Headquarters of Ajinomoto Fine-Techno in Gunma Prefecture; primary varnish production and R&D site.

nodes.label: Taiso Commerce Inc. (Taipei)
nodes.description: Ajinomoto subsidiary established in Taipei, Taiwan in 1988; regional activity hub for Taiwan customer base (ABF substrate makers).

nodes.label: Ajinomoto Shanghai Specialty Chemicals Co. Ltd.
nodes.description: Ajinomoto subsidiary established in Shanghai, China in 2018; activity hub for Chinese customers.
nodes.norm_label: ajinomoto shanghai specialty chemicals co. ltd.

nodes.label: AFT-USA (Silicon Valley)
nodes.description: Ajinomoto Fine-Techno USA subsidiary established in Silicon Valley, California in 2015; North America activity hub providing access to globally leading-edge semiconductor industry information.

nodes.label: AFT Future Creation Center
nodes.description: Established 2016; facility for envisioning and co-creating the future with customers through collaboration on value creation.

nodes.label: ABF Varnish
nodes.description: Liquid epoxy-based resin varnish produced by Ajinomoto; precursor material that film converters coat onto carrier film to produce finished ABF sheets. Upstream stage of ABF value chain.

nodes.label: Semiconductor Package Substrate (Build-up Substrate)
nodes.description: Multilayer printed circuit board (build-up substrate) used as interposer between semiconductor die and main PCB. Manufactured using build-up method: core material + sequentially laminated build-up layers (ABF) with laser-drilled vias and copper interconnects. Used in flip-chip plastic packages.

nodes.label: Flip-Chip Plastic Package
nodes.description: Semiconductor packaging technology that replaced wire bonding + ceramic packages in latter half of 1990s. Uses plastic build-up substrate with flip-chip interconnect; enables higher-density interconnects, higher-speed signal transmission, and lighter-weight substrates. ABF enabled mass proliferation of this technology in PCs.

nodes.label: Build-up PCB Manufacturing Method
nodes.description: Method of manufacturing multilayer PCBs by sequentially adding laminate layers to a core, laser-drilling vias, fabricating copper interconnects via plating. Enables fine copper patterning (L/S=10/10 um) and, in turn, smaller devices.

nodes.label: Laser Via Drilling
nodes.description: Process used in build-up method to create microvias between build-up layers of ABF-based substrates.

nodes.label: Semi-Additive Copper Plating
nodes.description: Copper interconnect fabrication method enabled by roughing ABF surface with oxidant, then applying copper plating. Allows fine-line (L/S=10/10 um) copper patterning on ABF insulating layer.

nodes.label: Server CPU Application
nodes.description: Server CPUs and GPUs for data center / cloud computing workloads. Identified as key growth driver for ABF from 2013 onward with cloud services market expansion; 2017-2022 CAGR of 7.2% forecast for data center server units.

nodes.label: PC CPU Application
nodes.description: Desktop/laptop CPU packaging; historical anchor application for ABF from 1999 launch through 2007 growth period. Market became stagnant after 2008 with smartphone/tablet rise.

nodes.label: GPU Application
nodes.description: Graphics processing units; driver of ABF logic IC market growth through 2019.

nodes.label: ASIC / FPGA (Network) Application
nodes.description: Application-specific integrated circuits and field-programmable gate arrays, particularly for networking equipment. Projected to drive ABF market growth alongside server CPUs/GPUs.

nodes.label: Game Console Application
nodes.description: Gaming console SoCs; cited as a non-PC ABF application category in the FY2019 ratio-by-use breakdown.

nodes.label: 5G / High-Frequency Communications Application
nodes.description: 5G wireless communication infrastructure requiring low dielectric tangent (low Df) substrates. Growth driver for ABF sales in 2019+ alongside server applications; motivates low-Df ABF variant development.

nodes.label: Data Center / Cloud Computing Application
nodes.description: Cloud services market expansion driving steady growth in installed data center servers and communication infrastructure, pulling ABF demand.

nodes.label: AI Accelerator / HPC Application
nodes.description: High-performance computing and AI accelerator packages (GPUs, TPUs, custom ASICs) requiring large-body flip-chip BGA substrates with many ABF build-up layers. Implied growth vector from ASIC/GPU trend cited in presentation.

nodes.label: HBM Stacking / 2.5D Interposer Application
nodes.description: Advanced packaging with HBM stacks on silicon interposer / organic substrate; ABF remains the interlayer dielectric in the organic substrate beneath the interposer. Implied inclusion within broader server CPU/AI accelerator growth, not explicitly named in 2019 deck.

nodes.label: Semiconductor Substrate Makers (Circuit Makers)
nodes.description: Direct customers of Ajinomoto Fine-Techno in the value chain. Referred to as 'Circuit makers' in the value chain slide; they purchase ABF and perform pre-processing, laminating, drilling, plating to produce build-up substrates supplied to CPU makers.

nodes.label: Ibiden Co., Ltd.
nodes.description: Japanese leading manufacturer of IC package substrates (FC-BGA) using ABF as build-up dielectric; historically a primary ABF customer for Intel CPU substrates.

nodes.label: Shinko Electric Industries
nodes.description: Japanese IC substrate manufacturer; major FC-BGA substrate supplier and ABF consumer.

nodes.label: Unimicron Technology Corp.
nodes.description: Taiwanese IC substrate and PCB manufacturer; major consumer of ABF for FC-BGA substrates.

nodes.label: Kinsus Interconnect Technology
nodes.description: Taiwanese IC substrate maker (ASE Group affiliate); ABF customer for FC-BGA substrates.

nodes.label: Nan Ya Printed Circuit Board
nodes.description: Taiwanese substrate manufacturer (Formosa Plastics Group); ABF consumer for IC substrates.

nodes.label: Samsung Electro-Mechanics (SEMCO)
nodes.description: Korean substrate manufacturer (Samsung affiliate); ABF consumer for FC-BGA IC substrates.

nodes.label: CPU Makers (End Semiconductor Customers)
nodes.description: Downstream end customers in ABF value chain identified as 'CPU makers' — semiconductor device companies that mount dies into ABF-based flip-chip packages produced by substrate makers.

nodes.label: Samsung Electronics (Semiconductor)
nodes.description: End customer for ABF-based substrates via Samsung Electro-Mechanics supply; produces mobile AP, HBM, and logic chips.

nodes.label: ASE Technology Holding
nodes.description: World's largest OSAT (outsourced semiconductor assembly & test); mounts semiconductor dies onto ABF-based substrates.

nodes.label: PC Makers
nodes.description: Downstream consumers of CPUs mounted with ABF substrates; completing the value chain from chemical makers to consumers.

nodes.label: Film Converters (Coating Contractors)
nodes.description: Outsourced coating companies that coat ABF varnish onto carrier film in the asset-light value chain. Includes F-LINE Corporation (Niigata) and a Gunma Prefecture contractor.

nodes.label: Logistics Companies (Low-Temperature Distribution)
nodes.description: Outsourced logistics providers that handle low-temperature distribution of ABF (resin products require cold-chain shipping to prevent premature curing).

nodes.label: Chemical Feedstock Suppliers
nodes.description: Upstream chemical makers supplying raw materials (resins, solvents, fillers, flame retardants) used in ABF varnish formulation.

nodes.label: Circuit-Maker Process Suppliers (Pre-processing, Laminating, Drilling, Plating)
nodes.description: Equipment and chemical suppliers to circuit makers for ABF downstream processing: pre-processing chemicals, laminating equipment, laser drilling systems, plating chemistry.

nodes.description: Japanese competitor offering build-up film materials / interlayer insulating films for semiconductor packaging; implied competitor to ABF in build-up dielectric market.

nodes.label: Taiyo Holdings
nodes.description: Japanese competitor specializing in solder resist and dielectric materials for PCB/IC substrates; competes adjacent to ABF dielectric space.

nodes.label: Resonac (formerly Showa Denko / Hitachi Chemical)
nodes.description: Japanese competitor producing build-up film / dielectric resins for IC substrates; notable alternative supplier to ABF.

nodes.label: S.A.V.E. Sales Approach

nodes.label: CXL 1.0 / 1.1 Specification
nodes.description: Initial release (March 2019). Direct-attached device, 32 GT/s link rate, 68-byte flit mode, single-node coherent interconnect with load/store semantics and hardware-enforced cache coherence. CXL 1.1 (June 2019) added compliance and interoperability.
nodes.source: Revision History table; CXL 4.0 Webinar timeline

nodes.label: CXL 2.0 Specification
nodes.description: Released October 2020. Added single-level switching, memory pooling with MLDs, managed hot-plug, persistent memory support, Global Persistent Flush (GPF), link-level encryption (CXL IDE), Fabric Manager API, and telemetry. Fully backward compatible with CXL 1.1/1.0.

nodes.label: CXL 3.0 Specification
nodes.description: Released August 2022. Doubled bandwidth to 64 GT/s using PAM-4 signaling on PCIe 6.0 PHY. Introduced multi-level switching up to 4K ports, Port-Based Routing (PBR) fabric, Back-Invalidate Snoop (BISnp), HDM-DB coherence, direct peer-to-peer to HDM memory, shared memory across virtual hierarchies, and 256B flit format.

nodes.label: CXL 3.1 Specification
nodes.description: Released November 2023. Added CXL Fabric capabilities, Global Integrated Memory (GIM), Trusted-Execution-Environment (TEE) Security Protocol (TSP), and host-to-host communication.

nodes.label: CXL 3.2 Specification
nodes.description: Released December 2024. Security, compliance, and CXL Memory Device enhancements; memory expander enhancements (up to 32-bit metadata, RAS capability).

nodes.label: CXL 4.0 Specification
nodes.description: Released November 2025 (evaluation copy dated August 13, 2025; version 1.0). Doubles data rate to 128 GT/s using PCIe 7.0 Base Specification PHY; uses same 256B flit arrangement, FEC, and CRC as CXL 3.0. Introduces CXL Bundled Ports for logical aggregation of multiple CXL ports (Type 1/2/3 accelerators), native x2 width support, up to 4 retimers for channel extension, optical link support via PCIe 7.0, and memory RAS enhancements (granular CVME events, host-initiated Post Package Repair, flexible memory sparing).

nodes.label: CXL.io
nodes.description: PCIe-based non-coherent I/O protocol with enhancements for accelerator support. Required for all CXL implementations; used for discovery, enumeration, configuration, error reporting, interrupts, DMA, ATS, and peer-to-peer access to CXL memory via Unordered I/O (UIO).

nodes.label: CXL.cache
nodes.description: Agent coherency protocol that supports device caching of host memory. Optional protocol for devices that need fully coherent caches (Type 1 and Type 2 devices). Uses D2H Request, D2H Response, D2H Data, H2D Request, H2D Response, H2D Data channels.

nodes.label: CXL.mem
nodes.description: Memory access protocol that supports device-attached memory (HDM — Host-managed Device Memory). Used by Type 2 and Type 3 devices. Introduced Back-Invalidate Snoop (BISnp) channel in CXL 3.0 for HDM-DB coherence, and QoS Telemetry with 2-bit DevLoad field. Supports M2S Request, M2S RwD, S2M NDR, S2M DRS, S2M BISnp, M2S BIRsp.

nodes.label: CXL Type 1 Device
nodes.description: Accelerator/caching device with fully coherent cache but no device-attached memory. Uses CXL.io + CXL.cache protocols. Example use cases: NIC atomics, PGAS NICs, Smart NICs, complex atomics. Supports producer-consumer models that don't work with standard PCIe ordering; cache size limited by host's snoop-filter capacity.

nodes.label: CXL Type 2 Device
nodes.description: Accelerator with device-attached memory that negotiates all three protocols (CXL.io, CXL.cache, CXL.mem). Device memory (DDR, HBM, GDDR) is exposed as Host-managed Device Memory (HDM-D or HDM-DB). Example: GPGPU, FPGA, dense-computation accelerators. Implements bias-based coherency (Host Bias vs Device Bias) for HDM-D; HDM-DB uses Back-Invalidate Snoop channel.

nodes.label: CXL Type 3 Device
nodes.description: Memory expander/memory buffer. Supports CXL.io + CXL.mem protocols (no CXL.cache). Primary use: passive memory expansion (HDM-H) where device does not manipulate memory content while exposed to host. HDM-DB variant supports accelerator-like behavior and peer UIO access. Can be partitioned into up to 16 Logical Devices (MLD). Supports memory pooling, memory sharing (FAM/G-FAM), and 2LM (two-level memory) tiering.

nodes.label: Flex Bus
nodes.description: A flexible high-speed port that can be dynamically configured to support either native PCIe protocol or CXL over a high-bandwidth off-package link. Selection occurs during link training via alternate protocol negotiation. Uses PCIe electricals, compatible with PCIe retimers and form factors. Supports x16, x8, x4, x2, x1 link widths; signaling rates 8/16/32/64/128 GT/s.

nodes.label: CXL Bundled Port (BPD)
nodes.description: CXL 4.0 feature: logical aggregation of multiple CXL ports of an accelerator device (Type 1, Type 2, Type 3 non-memory). A Bundle exposes at least one standard full-capability Port and any number of Streamlined Ports. Streamlined Ports are area/power-optimized for data bandwidth expansion, 256B Flit mode only, optimized for UIO. Each port exposes an SLD-B (Single Logical Device of a Bundle).
nodes.source: Section 1.4.1 paragraph on CXL 4.0; Section 2.10; Webinar slides 16-20

nodes.label: Host-managed Device Memory (HDM)
nodes.description: Device memory exposed to the host and managed by the host coherence domain. Three variants: HDM-H (Host-only coherent, Type 3 memory expander), HDM-D (Device coherent using CXL.cache bias model), HDM-DB (Device coherent using Back-Invalidate Snoop on CXL.mem; required for 256B flit devices).

nodes.label: Multi-Logical Device (MLD)
nodes.description: Type 3 device partitioned into up to 16 isolated Logical Devices (LDs), each identified by a 16-bit LD-ID. Each LD appears as a Type 3 device to its bound Virtual Hierarchy (VH). One LD is reserved for the Fabric Manager (FMLD, LD-ID FFFFh). Enables memory pooling.

nodes.label: Global FAM (G-FAM) / GFD
nodes.description: Global Fabric-Attached Memory. Highly scalable form of FAM that presents HDM to hosts without using Logical Devices, instead using Port-Based Routing (PBR) with Source PID (SPID). G-FAM devices (GFDs) support multiple host/peer interfaces on one or more links. Enables pooled or shared memory at fabric scale.

nodes.label: Fabric Manager (FM)
nodes.description: Entity separate from switch or host firmware that controls binding and management of pooled CXL ports and devices. Introduced in CXL 2.0. Standardized API for switch orchestration in CXL 3.0/3.1 fabrics. Fabric Manager API over MCTP Binding Specification DSP0234.

nodes.label: CXL Integrity and Data Encryption (CXL IDE)
nodes.description: Link-level encryption scheme used for securing CXL protocol flits across the link. Uses AES-GCM per NIST SP 800-38D. Introduced in CXL 2.0.
nodes.norm_label: cxl integrity and data encryption (cxl ide)

nodes.label: TEE Security Protocol (TSP)
nodes.description: CXL-defined TEE Security Protocol. Collection of requirements and interfaces that allow memory devices to be utilized for confidential computing. Leverages PCI-SIG TDISP, SPDM, and DSM (Device Security Manager) / TSM (TEE Security Manager).
nodes.source: Terminology Table (TSP, TSM, DSM, TDISP entries)

nodes.label: PCI Express Base Specification 7.0
nodes.description: PCIe Base Specification Revision 7.0. Provides the PHY used by CXL 4.0 at 128 GT/s, including its FEC, CRC, and optical link support. Published by PCI-SIG.

nodes.label: PCI Express 6.0
nodes.description: PCIe 6.0 provides the 64 GT/s PAM-4 PHY leveraged by CXL 3.0/3.1/3.2.
nodes.source: CXL 3.0 description in Section 1.4.1 references PCIe Base PHY; PCIe 6.0 is the industry standard at 64 GT/s

nodes.label: PCI-SIG
nodes.description: Standards organization that publishes the PCI Express Base Specification and related specifications (CEM, ATS, DOE, AER, TDISP). CXL is built on top of PCIe electricals and reuses much of the PCIe protocol stack.

nodes.label: DMTF (Distributed Management Task Force)
nodes.description: Standards body for DSP0234 (Fabric Manager API over MCTP), DSP0236 (MCTP Base), DSP0274 (SPDM), DSP0281 (CXL Type 3 CCI over MCTP). CXL references numerous DMTF specifications for management and security.

nodes.label: UEFI Forum
nodes.description: Publishes ACPI Specification (6.5+), CDAT Specification, and UEFI Specification, all referenced by CXL for coherent device attribute tables, memory affinity structures, and firmware interfaces.

nodes.label: JEDEC
nodes.description: Publishes DDR5 Specification (JESD79-5), referenced by CXL for memory device specifications.

nodes.label: Xconn Technologies
nodes.description: CXL member; JP Jiang represents Xconn. Develops CXL switches for memory disaggregation and fabric topologies.

nodes.label: SAP SE
nodes.description: Enterprise software vendor; collaborated with Intel on TPCC benchmark studies showing CXL-pooled memory works well for database workloads with negligible performance degradation even with switches.

nodes.label: Memory Pooling
nodes.description: Data center use case where CXL-attached memory (Type 3 MLD or GFD) is disaggregated from compute and dynamically allocated across servers. Reduces memory stranding, improves utilization, lowers TCO. Microsoft Pond paper (ASPLOS 2023) showed 9% DRAM savings.
nodes.source: CXL 4.0 Webinar challenge 3 slides; ecosystem health slide

nodes.label: Coherent Memory Sharing
nodes.description: CXL 3.0+ use case where multiple hosts concurrently access a single HDM region (Shared FAM) with hardware-enforced coherence across copies in host caches. Enables collaborative processing and in-memory message passing across virtual hierarchies.

nodes.label: Accelerator Cache Coherency
nodes.description: CXL enables accelerators (GPUs, FPGAs, NICs) to participate in host cache coherence via CXL.cache and to expose accelerator-local memory as HDM. Eliminates high-bandwidth copies between host and device memory that plague traditional PCIe-attached accelerators.

nodes.label: Composable Disaggregated Fabric
nodes.description: CXL 3.0+ use case: spine/leaf switched fabric at rack/pod scale with Port-Based Routing (PBR), enabling fine-grained data sharing and message passing across distributed compute, memory, NIC, and accelerator enclosures.
